title: The thumb-pivot cut
subtitle: Short, controlled cuts for fine shaping and detail work
excerpt: The thumb-pivot cut is the precision companion to the push cut. Off-hand thumb pressed against the back of the blade or knife hand provides a pivot point that limits blade travel and gives very fine control over thin slices. Used for refining curves, trimming corners, and all close-detail shaping.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for grip variations in hand-tool carving.

glossaryTerms.definition: A carving cut in which the off-hand thumb presses against the back of the blade or the knife hand, acting as a pivot and limit to blade travel. Produces short, controlled cuts for fine shaping.
A carving cut in which the blade travels away from the body, driven by forearm extension. The default carving cut.

content.text: The
thumb-pivot cut

is what you reach for when the push cut is too large and uncontrolled for the work at hand. Refining the curve on the back of a spoon bowl, trimming the corner off a chip-carving pattern, or paring a thin sliver from the inside of a joint: all of these require a cut whose travel is limited to a few millimetres. The thumb acts as both a pivot and a travel stop, giving the maker very fine control over how far the blade moves.
When to use it
The thumb-pivot cut suits any situation where the
push cut

would remove too much material or travel past the intended stopping point. Curved surfaces, fine profile work, and any cut that needs to stop at a specific point rather than exit off the end of the workpiece are all candidates. It also works well near the tip of a spoon handle or any narrow section where the long stroke of a push cut would split the piece.
What you need

A sloyd knife with a sharp edge. A piece of scrap green or seasoned wood to practice the cut on before applying it to a project. The technique requires no additional tools beyond what the push cut uses.
The technique
Grip the work and knife

Hold the workpiece in the non-dominant hand with the four-finger grip: thumb on top, four fingers underneath. Hold the knife in the dominant hand with the handle in a closed fist. Now bring both hands together so the off-hand thumb presses against the back of the blade, just behind the cutting edge, or against the back of the knife-hand knuckles. The off-hand thumb becomes the pivot point and the travel limit.
Make the cut

Place the blade on the wood at the starting point of the cut. Press the off-hand thumb firmly against the back of the blade or knife hand. Now close the knife hand in a controlled squeeze, drawing the blade edge toward the thumb pivot. The off-hand thumb prevents the blade from travelling past the pivot point. The resulting cut is very short, typically 5 to 15 mm, and very controlled. Reposition and repeat.

The cut should feel like a gentle paring action, not a push. The force comes from closing the knife-hand grip, not from pushing the whole hand forward. If you are pushing forward, you have reverted to a push cut and lost the control benefit of the pivot.
What it looks like when it is right

A correct thumb-pivot cut produces a very thin, precise shaving. The surface left behind is clean and slightly curved if the blade tracked along a curved surface. The blade stops exactly where the thumb pivot limits it, not a millimetre further. You can take five successive pivot cuts and end up with a flat plane within a tight radius, which is what makes this cut the standard for refining the outside of a spoon bowl.
Common mistakes
Not pressing the thumb firmly enough against the blade back. A loose pivot lets the blade travel further than intended. The thumb must press firmly against the metal to work as a genuine stop.
Starting with too steep a blade angle. The pivot cut works best at a shallow angle, 10 to 15 degrees from the surface. A steep angle takes too thick a chip for the control the pivot provides.

Using it on end grain without first making a stop cut. On end grain the blade can run with the grain past the stopping point even with the thumb pivot in place. Make a stop cut perpendicular to the surface first to define the boundary, then use the pivot cut to pare toward it.
